questions.question: How do people catch Legionnaires' disease?
options: By breathing in tiny droplets of water (an aerosol) that contain legionella bacteria
By close contact with someone who already has the illness
By drinking a glass of ordinary tap water
By touching a surface an unwell person has touched

questions.question: What kind of illness is Legionnaires' disease?
options: A serious form of pneumonia, which is a lung infection
A mild skin rash caused by hard water
A type of common cold that clears on its own
A stomach bug caused by swallowing dirty water

questions.question: Which statement about catching Legionnaires' disease is correct?
options: You cannot catch it simply by drinking water
It can only be caught outdoors near rivers and lakes
It is caught by eating contaminated food
It spreads easily from person to person through coughing

questions.question: In roughly which temperature range does legionella multiply fastest?
options: Between 0 and 10 degrees Celsius
Between 20 and 45 degrees Celsius
Between 90 and 100 degrees Celsius
Between 60 and 80 degrees Celsius

questions.question: Above roughly which temperature is legionella killed?
options: Around 45 degrees Celsius
Around 60 degrees Celsius
Around 30 degrees Celsius
Around 20 degrees Celsius

questions.question: Below roughly which temperature does legionella stay dormant?
options: Below about 45 degrees Celsius
Below about 20 degrees Celsius
Below about 55 degrees Celsius
Below about 60 degrees Celsius

questions.question: In a care setting, what is the most common source of breathable droplets?
options: A kettle used to make hot drinks
A radiator in a resident's room
The shower, which sprays warm water into the air
A closed window

questions.question: Why are showers considered a particular legionella risk in care settings?
options: They are the only place legionella bacteria can grow
The water in them is always too hot for bacteria to survive
They spray warm water into the air as fine droplets that can be breathed deep into the lungs
They use more water than any other fitting in the building

questions.question: Why are little-used taps and showers a legionella concern?
options: They are cleaned far more often than outlets in daily use
They are usually connected to a completely separate water supply
Water sits still and warms up in them, letting bacteria multiply before the outlet is next used
Bacteria cannot survive in water that is left standing

questions.question: What is described as the single most important control against legionella?
options: The colour of the pipework
The height of the water tank
The brand of tap fitted
Temperature

questions.question: In practice, hot water is usually stored at around which temperature?
options: Around 45 degrees
Around 35 degrees
Around 60 degrees
Around 20 degrees

questions.question: Cold water is generally kept below about which temperature?
options: Below about 20 degrees
Below about 40 degrees
Below about 60 degrees
Below about 50 degrees

questions.question: Why are mixing valves often fitted near showers and basins?
options: To add extra bacteria-killing chemicals to the water
To bring very hot water down to a safe temperature at the point of use, so it does not scald
To make the water flow faster
To keep the cold water warm

questions.question: What does flushing an outlet mean?
options: Running a tap or shower for a set time so fresh water replaces the standing water
Turning the outlet off for a set period so no water can move
Adding descaler to the water tank
Removing the tap completely and fitting a new one

questions.question: How often are outlets not in regular use often flushed?
options: Every few years
Only when a resident complains
Once a year
Weekly

questions.question: Why does your service ask you to descale and clean showerheads regularly?
options: It is only about appearance and has no effect on safety
Clean showerheads make the water come out hotter
Scale and biofilm feed and shelter legionella, so removing them keeps the spray cleaner and safer
It removes the need to keep hot water hot or cold water cold

questions.question: Which of these provides food and shelter that helps legionella grow?
options: Clean, fast-moving water
Cold water below 20 degrees
Scale, rust, sludge and biofilm inside pipes and fittings
Very hot water above 60 degrees

questions.question: Where are legionella bacteria found naturally, usually in numbers too low to cause harm?
options: In dry soil and sand
In the air we breathe outdoors
In rivers and lakes
In sea salt

questions.question: Who has overall legal responsibility for controlling legionella risk in the building?
options: The care worker on shift
The duty holder
A visiting relative
Each individual resident

questions.question: Who is the named person who manages the day-to-day legionella controls?
options: The newest member of staff
The window cleaner
The responsible person
The resident's GP

questions.question: Who carries out and manages the legionella risk assessment?
options: The duty holder and the responsible person
Nobody; it is not required
The residents themselves
Every care worker individually

questions.question: A resident develops a high temperature, muscle aches, a cough and breathlessness. What should you do?
options: Report it promptly to a senior colleague or nurse so that medical help can be arranged
Decide yourself that it is Legionnaires' disease and tell the resident
Assume it cannot be serious because chest infections are common
Wait several days to see whether the symptoms clear up on their own

questions.question: Which best describes your role in controlling legionella?
options: Deciding which controls the service needs and setting water temperatures
Doing nothing, because legionella is entirely someone else's responsibility
Following the water safety plan, carrying out assigned tasks, recording them and reporting concerns
Carrying out the legionella risk assessment for the whole building

questions.question: You notice that a hot tap only runs warm rather than genuinely hot. What is the right response?
options: Set the hot water temperature higher yourself
Ignore it, as water temperature is never important
Report it to the responsible person or a senior colleague as a warning sign
Tell residents to stop using all taps in the building

questions.question: How does Legionnaires' disease often begin before it affects the lungs?
options: With a bad case of flu-like symptoms such as high temperature, chills, headache and aching muscles
With a sudden skin rash all over the body
With immediate, total loss of the ability to breathe
With no symptoms at all until recovery

questions.question: Legionnaires' disease can be passed from one person to another, like a cold or flu.
options: True
False

questions.question: Keeping hot water hot and cold water cold helps control legionella because the bacteria thrive in warm, in-between temperatures.
options: True
False

questions.question: You can catch Legionnaires' disease simply by drinking a glass of contaminated water.
options: True
False

questions.question: Legionella multiplies fastest in water that is warm and stagnant.
options: True
False

questions.question: This e-learning course replaces your service's legionella risk assessment.
options: True
False

questions.question: Legionella bacteria are found naturally in rivers and lakes, usually in numbers too low to cause harm.
options: True
False

questions.question: As a care worker, you are expected to carry out the legionella risk assessment yourself.
options: True
False

questions.question: Smokers and heavy drinkers are at higher risk of becoming seriously ill from legionella.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following people are at higher risk of becoming seriously ill from legionella? (Select all that apply)
options: Older people, particularly over 45
Healthy young adults with no health conditions who never go near water
People with existing lung or chest conditions
People whose immune system is weakened by illness or medication

questions.question: Which conditions does legionella need in order to multiply? (Select all that apply)
options: Warm water, roughly between 20 and 45 degrees
Water that is stagnant or standing still
Scale, rust, sludge or biofilm to feed on
Clean, fast-moving water kept genuinely hot or cold

questions.question: Which of these can create a breathable spray that could carry legionella into the lungs? (Select all that apply)
options: Spray taps
Drinking a glass of water
Showers
Hoses and sprinklers

questions.question: Which of these should you report to the responsible person or a senior colleague? (Select all that apply)
options: Hot water that only runs warm, or cold water that feels warm
A showerhead heavily coated in scale
A tap that runs clear, genuinely hot and is used every day
Water that looks discoloured or smells unusual

questions.question: Which of these are symptoms of Legionnaires' disease described in the course? (Select all that apply)
options: Confusion, tiredness, and sometimes sickness or diarrhoea
A cough, breathlessness and sometimes chest pain
High temperature, chills, headache and aching muscles
A broken bone that heals within a day

questions.question: Which of these are your responsibilities as a care worker within the water safety plan? (Select all that apply)
options: Carrying out simple assigned tasks such as flushing and cleaning
Deciding which controls the service needs and setting water temperatures
Reporting any concerns or symptoms straight away
Keeping accurate records of the tasks you complete

questions.question: Which of these are areas where water can stagnate and become a legionella concern? (Select all that apply)
options: A tap that is used many times every day
Little-used outlets in empty rooms or spare bathrooms
Stored water in tanks and cylinders
Dead legs, lengths of pipe leading to seldom-used fittings

questions.question: Legionnaires' disease is a serious form of ___.
Legionella multiplies fastest in water that is warm and ___, roughly between 20 and 45 degrees.
The named person who manages the day-to-day legionella controls is the ___ person.
Running a little-used tap or shower for a set time so fresh water replaces the standing water is called ___.
People catch Legionnaires' disease by breathing in a fine spray or mist of water called an ___.
